record_office_terminology.land_records: Department of Land Management (land title records, surveys, and cadastral data -- single territorial office in Hagatna)
record_office_terminology.probate: Superior Court of Guam (wills, estates, guardianships; formerly the Island Court under the Naval Government)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Superior Court of Guam (marriage licenses; Catholic Church marriages were the only form during Spanish period 1668-1898)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1901 (births and deaths under US Naval Government; marriages from 1901 civilly, from 1668 in Catholic Church records)

burned_county_authority: Richard F. Taitano Micronesian Area Research Center (MARC), University of Guam -- Guam experienced catastrophic record losses during the Japanese occupation (1941-1944) and the Battle of Guam (1944). MARC is the primary institution preserving and reconstructing Guam's documentary heritage, holding microfilm copies of Spanish colonial records from archives in Spain, surviving US Naval Government records, Japanese occupation-era documents, and oral history collections.
administrative_notes: Guam is an unincorporated territory of the United States located in the western Pacific Ocean, the largest and southernmost of the Mariana Islands. The indigenous Chamorro people have inhabited the island for approximately 4,000 years. Spain colonized Guam in 1668 and administered it as part of the Islas Marianas until ceding it to the United States after the Spanish-American War (Treaty of Paris, December 10, 1898). The US Navy governed Guam from 1899 to 1941. Japan invaded and occupied the island from December 1941 to July 1944, causing massive destruction of records and infrastructure. After liberation, the Organic Act of Guam (1950) established civilian self-government and granted US citizenship to residents. Guam is organized into 19 villages (municipalities) for local administration, but for genealogical purposes functions as a single island-wide jurisdiction. The Catholic Church has been central to Chamorro life since 1668, and church sacramental records are the primary genealogical source for the Spanish colonial period. Spain imposed a surname system on Chamorro families in the 1850s-1860s using an official catalog of approved names, which is critical context for understanding Chamorro family naming patterns. Researchers should be aware that many records are in Spanish (colonial era), Japanese (occupation period), and English/Chamorro (American era). The Richard F. Taitano Micronesian Area Research Center (MARC) at the University of Guam is the central research repository for Guam history and genealogy.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Guam experienced devastating record losses during the Japanese occupation (1941-1944) and the Battle of Guam (1944). Government buildings in Hagatna (then Agana) were destroyed, including offices holding civil records. The Japanese military confiscated or destroyed many documents. The subsequent American liberation campaign caused further destruction through bombing and combat. Catholic Church records survived somewhat better because parish priests concealed sacramental registers, but losses were still significant. Spanish colonial civil records were largely lost. Researchers must rely on reconstructed records, church registers, oral histories, and records held in overseas archives (Spain, NARA) to fill gaps.

core_repositories.source_name: Richard F. Taitano Micronesian Area Research Center (MARC)
core_repositories.description: The premier research repository for Guam and Micronesian history. Holdings include Spanish colonial documents and microfilm from the Archivo General de Indias (Seville) and Archivo Historico Nacional (Madrid), US Naval Government records (1899-1941), Japanese occupation materials, oral history collections (over 500 interviews with Chamorro elders and WWII survivors), photographs, maps, the Guam Recorder periodical (1924-1941), post-war land claims records, and genealogical files. MARC is named for Richard Flores Taitano, a Chamorro historian and cultural preservationist.

The territorial museum preserving Guam's cultural heritage from ancient Chamorro civilization through the present. Collections include archaeological artifacts, historical documents, photographs, and cultural materials. The museum's archives contain family histories, village records, and cultural documentation relevant to genealogical research. The Senator Antonio M. Palomo Guam Museum and Chamorro Educational Facility (its full name) emphasizes indigenous Chamorro history and traditions.

The Catholic Archdiocese of Agana (originally the Diocese of Agana, elevated to Archdiocese in 1984) maintains sacramental registers for all Guam parishes. Catholic records are the oldest and most complete genealogical source for Guam, beginning with the establishment of the first mission in 1668. Baptismal, marriage, burial, and confirmation records document virtually the entire Chamorro population during the Spanish period. The Chancery Office coordinates access to historical parish registers. Some registers have been microfilmed and copies are at MARC.

familysearch_collections.description: Limited but growing collections for Guam. FamilySearch has microfilmed some Catholic parish registers from Guam and holds microfilm of selected MARC collections. Available collections may include baptismal records, marriage records, and civil registration documents from the US Naval Government period. The FamilySearch catalog should be searched for 'Guam' and for individual parish names.

Research guide for Guam genealogy covering the island's unique record systems across Spanish colonial, US Naval Government, Japanese occupation, and modern American periods. Provides guidance on Catholic Church records, vital statistics, land records, and the Chamorro surname system. Links to available microfilm and digital collections.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Guam Recorder and Historical Periodicals -- MARC
newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Richard F. Taitano MARC, University of Guam
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: The Guam Recorder (1924-1941) was the island's principal English-language periodical during the Naval Government era, publishing news, vital statistics notices, government orders, and community events. MARC holds a complete run. Post-war newspapers include the Pacific Daily News (from 1950) and the Guam Daily Post. Newspaper notices for births, deaths, marriages, and legal proceedings supplement official records, especially for the post-WWII period.

military_records.source_name: WWII and Guam Military Records -- NARA
military_records.description: NARA holds US military records relating to Guam: the 1941 Japanese invasion, the 1944 liberation (Battle of Guam), post-war military government records, and records of the Island Command. Also holds Guam War Claims Commission records documenting civilian war damage claims with family names, property descriptions, and wartime experiences. Records of the US Naval Government of Guam (1899-1941) are in NARA Record Group 313. Post-war military base construction records document land condemnation affecting Chamorro families.

land_and_property.repository: Department of Land Management, Government of Guam, Hagatna
land_and_property.description: The Department of Land Management maintains land title records from the US Naval Government period (1899 forward). The Naval Government established a cadastral survey and title registration system replacing the informal Spanish-era system. Post-WWII land title restoration proceedings reconstructed ownership records destroyed during the war. The Chamorro Land Trust Commission administers ancestral lands. Land condemnation records for US military bases (comprising approximately one-third of Guam's land area) document displaced families and are at NARA and MARC.

vital_records.repository: Department of Public Health and Social Services, Office of Vital Statistics, Mangilao
vital_records.description: Birth and death certificates from 1901 (US Naval Government registration). Marriage licenses and certificates. Pre-1901 vital events are documented only in Catholic Church sacramental records. Records from 1901-1941 have gaps due to WWII destruction. Japanese occupation period (1941-1944) has minimal civil registration. Post-war delayed registrations were filed for births and deaths that occurred during the occupation. Modern vital records are centralized at the territorial level.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Catholic Parish Records and Cemeteries of Guam
church_and_cemetery.repository: Archdiocese of Agana / Individual parishes / MARC
church_and_cemetery.description: Catholic Church records are the foundation of Guam genealogy. The island has approximately 25 parishes, many tracing their origins to Spanish colonial missions. The Cathedral-Basilica of Dulce Nombre de Maria in Hagatna is the mother church (established 1669). Major parishes include San Dionisio (Umatac), Nuestra Senora de la Soledad (Inarajan), San Jose (Inarajan), and Santa Rosa (Agat). Each parish maintains its own cemetery with burial records. Village cemeteries document family burial patterns across generations. Some cemetery records have been transcribed and are at MARC.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Local genealogical organization assisting researchers with Chamorro family history. Provides guidance on navigating Catholic Church records, Spanish colonial documents, and the Chamorro surname system imposed by Spain in the 1850s-1860s. Connects researchers with oral history traditions and family knowledge essential for bridging record gaps caused by WWII destruction.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Archivo General de Indias -- Spanish Colonial Records for Guam
research_guides_and_societies.repository: Archivo General de Indias, Seville, Spain / MARC (microfilm)
research_guides_and_societies.description: The principal Spanish colonial archive holds records of the administration of the Islas Marianas (Mariana Islands) including Guam from 1668 to 1898. Documents include colonial censuses (padrones), land grants, church-state correspondence, military records, and administrative reports. MARC holds microfilm copies of many Guam-relevant documents. Records are in Spanish and require paleographic skills for older documents.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Guam has no extinct counties or jurisdictions in the traditional sense. The island has functioned as a single jurisdiction throughout its recorded history. Under Spanish colonial administration (1668-1898), Guam was governed as part of the Islas Marianas (Mariana Islands) with the governor resident in Hagatna (then Hagadna/Agana). The US Naval Government (1899-1941) continued single island-wide administration. The 19 villages (municipalities) that exist today have historical roots in the Spanish reduccion system, which consolidated scattered Chamorro settlements into organized villages near mission churches in the late 17th century. For genealogical purposes, records are organized by the island-wide jurisdiction, not by village, though Catholic parish records correspond to specific villages.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Chamorro Cultural Resources -- MARC / Guam Museum
native_american_and_tribal_records.repository: Richard F. Taitano MARC / Guam Museum / Guam Preservation Trust
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: Resources documenting Chamorro indigenous culture, oral traditions, and family histories. MARC holds extensive oral history collections including interviews with Chamorro elders recording family genealogies (usually called 'familias' in local tradition), village histories, and cultural practices. The Chamorro people maintained oral genealogical traditions before and alongside written records. Archaeological and anthropological studies at MARC document pre-contact Chamorro civilization (latte period, approximately 900-1668 CE). Understanding Chamorro kinship systems and clan structures (in Chamorro, 'familia' extends broadly) is essential for contextualizing genealogical research.
